abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
SVT in CTICU

Supraventricular tachycardia is a rapid rhythm arising above the ventricles, usually narrow-complex unless there is aberrancy or bundle branch block. In the CTICU, SVT reduces diastolic filling time, increases oxygen demand and may precipitate hypotension in patients with myocardial stunning, valve disease, RV dysfunction or ischaemia.

SVT — A tachyarrhythmia originating above the His-Purkinje system, commonly AVNRT, AVRT or atrial tachycardia, usually with a regular narrow QRS rate around 150–250/min.

Aberrancy — Rate-related or baseline conduction delay causing a supraventricular rhythm to appear broad-complex.

Pattern Likely diagnosis Bedside implication
Regular narrow tachycardia 150–250/min SVT, atrial flutter with fixed block, atrial tachycardia. Assess stability and obtain 12-lead; consider vagal/adenosine pathway only if appropriate and prescribed.
Irregular narrow tachycardia AF or multifocal atrial tachycardia more likely. Avoid assuming SVT; management differs.
Regular broad tachycardia VT until proven otherwise. In CTICU, treat broad regular tachycardia as VT unless expert review confirms otherwise.
SVT with MAP <60, ischaemia or pulmonary oedema Unstable tachyarrhythmia. Escalate for synchronised cardioversion.

Escalate immediately for any SVT with hypotension, altered consciousness, chest pain/ischaemia, acute pulmonary oedema, shock, or uncertain broad-complex rhythm.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Mechanism and haemodynamic consequences

SVT commonly involves re-entry circuits. AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia uses dual AV nodal pathways; AV re-entrant tachycardia uses an accessory pathway; atrial tachycardia arises from an atrial focus. The ECG often shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia with absent, hidden or retrograde P waves. Atrial flutter with 2:1 block can mimic SVT at around 150/min and should be considered, especially after cardiac surgery.

The haemodynamic harm of SVT is rate-related. At 180–220/min, diastolic filling time becomes very short. Stroke volume falls, coronary perfusion time falls, myocardial oxygen demand rises, and arterial pressure may collapse. After cardiac surgery the myocardium may be stunned and less able to compensate. Valve patients and patients with diastolic dysfunction may deteriorate quickly.

Stable SVT management may include vagal manoeuvres, adenosine, beta-blockade, calcium-channel blockers or amiodarone depending on context, contraindications and local protocols. In CTICU, adenosine should be used with monitoring, IV access, immediate flush and readiness for transient asystole or bronchospasm. In irregular wide-complex rhythms or pre-excited AF, AV nodal blockers can be dangerous; senior input is essential.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls

A regular tachycardia at 150/min after cardiac surgery may be atrial flutter with 2:1 block, not simple SVT. Look carefully for flutter waves, especially in inferior leads and V1.

The patient’s blood pressure and perfusion decide urgency more than the rhythm label. A narrow tachycardia can be life-threatening in a stunned ventricle.

A broad-complex tachycardia in a post-cardiac surgery patient is VT until proven otherwise. Do not reassure yourself that it is SVT with aberrancy unless an expert has reviewed it.

Adenosine may reveal flutter waves without terminating the rhythm. This is diagnostically useful but can be alarming because of transient AV block or pauses.

SVT after surgery often has a trigger. If you convert the rhythm but leave potassium 3.2 mmol/L, pain uncontrolled and noradrenaline climbing, it may recur.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In

Defibrillator and rhythm capture

Keep defibrillator pads available for unstable tachyarrhythmias. For conscious stable SVT, ensure continuous ECG, arterial pressure and oxygen saturation monitoring during drug therapy. Print rhythm strips before, during and after adenosine if used; the transient AV block can reveal atrial activity.

Equipment task Why it matters How to do it
12-lead ECG before treatment if stable Differentiates SVT, flutter, VT, ischaemia and pre-excitation. Acquire rapidly without delaying cardioversion in instability.
Continuous arterial waveform Shows beat-to-beat haemodynamic tolerance. Watch pulse pressure and MAP during tachycardia and conversion.
Adenosine delivery setup Drug half-life is seconds. Use proximal IV, rapid push, immediate flush, continuous ECG.
Sync mode for unstable SVT Avoids shock on T wave. Confirm sync markers on QRS before shock.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Targets and Numbers

Parameter Typical value CT-ICU interpretation
SVT rate Often 150–250/min. Higher rates reduce filling and increase oxygen demand.
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block Often near 150/min. Consider if regular narrow tachycardia is exactly around 150/min.
Unstable tachycardia criteria Hypotension, shock, ischaemia, pulmonary oedema, altered consciousness. Synchronised cardioversion pathway.
QRS width Narrow <120 ms; broad ≥120 ms. Broad regular tachycardia should be treated as VT until proven otherwise.
Adenosine effect Transient AV block/asystole seconds. Must be monitored; resuscitation equipment ready.
MAP Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered. Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours. Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support. After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk. Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk. Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180/min with MAP 50 mmHg is best treated as:
options: Unstable tachyarrhythmia requiring urgent escalation/cardioversion pathway
A rhythm to observe for 6 hours
A reason to remove arterial line
quiz.explanation: Narrow-complex does not mean safe. Hypotension makes the tachyarrhythmia unstable.

quiz.question: A regular narrow rhythm at about 150/min after cardiac surgery may actually be:
quiz.explanation: Flutter with 2:1 AV conduction commonly produces a ventricular rate near 150/min and may mimic SVT.

quiz.question: Why is a regular broad-complex tachycardia treated as VT until proven otherwise?
options: Misclassifying VT as SVT can delay life-saving treatment and some AV nodal blockers may harm
quiz.explanation: Broad-complex tachycardia may be VT, especially in structural heart disease/post-surgery. Treating as SVT can be dangerous.
